title: Guru Granth Sahib
subtitle: The Holy Scripture of Sikhism
description: The central religious scripture of Sikhism, regarded by Sikhs as the final, sovereign, and living Guru. It was compiled by Guru Arjan and later expanded by Guru Gobind Singh.
summary: The Guru Granth Sahib contains hymns and verses composed by six Sikh Gurus and various Hindu and Muslim saints. It emphasizes monotheism, equality, selfless service, meditation on God's name, and the rejection of caste and ritualism.
originalLanguage: Punjabi, Sanskrit, Hindi, Persian, Braj Bhasha

chapters.title: Japji Sahib — The Morning Prayer (Guru Nanak)

verses.text: Ik Onkar – There is One God. Truth is His Name. He is the Creator. He is without fear. He is without hatred. Timeless is His form. Beyond birth. Self-existent. By Guru's grace.
verses.explanation: This verse from Japji Sahib, the morning prayer of Guru Nanak, opens with Mool Mantra: 'Ik Onkar — There is One God.' It establishes the core Sikh belief in the oneness of the Creator, who is truth, fearless, timeless, and accessible through the Guru's grace.

verses.text: He is beyond time. He cannot be described. By hearing, seeing, and speaking, He is purified.
verses.explanation: This verse from Japji Sahib, the morning prayer of Guru Nanak, opens with Mool Mantra: 'Ik Onkar — There is One God.' It establishes the core Sikh belief in the oneness of the Creator, who is truth, fearless, timeless, and accessible through the Guru's grace.

verses.text: By thinking, He cannot be reduced to thought, even by thinking hundreds of thousands of times. By remaining silent, inner silence is not obtained, even by remaining lovingly absorbed deep within.
verses.explanation: This verse from Japji Sahib, the morning prayer of Guru Nanak, opens with Mool Mantra: 'Ik Onkar — There is One God.' It establishes the core Sikh belief in the oneness of the Creator, who is truth, fearless, timeless, and accessible through the Guru's grace.

verses.text: The hunger of the hungry is appeased by eating; even by eating hundreds of thousands of tons of food, hunger is not appeased.
verses.explanation: This verse from Japji Sahib, the morning prayer of Guru Nanak, opens with Mool Mantra: 'Ik Onkar — There is One God.' It establishes the core Sikh belief in the oneness of the Creator, who is truth, fearless, timeless, and accessible through the Guru's grace.

verses.text: Through the Guru's Teachings, the Great One is known. He cannot be known by boasting.
verses.explanation: This verse from Japji Sahib, the morning prayer of Guru Nanak, opens with Mool Mantra: 'Ik Onkar — There is One God.' It establishes the core Sikh belief in the oneness of the Creator, who is truth, fearless, timeless, and accessible through the Guru's grace.

verses.text: There is no shortage of those who speak; who speaks the truth? Only that person is honored, O Lord, who does what You say.
verses.explanation: This verse from Japji Sahib, the morning prayer of Guru Nanak, opens with Mool Mantra: 'Ik Onkar — There is One God.' It establishes the core Sikh belief in the oneness of the Creator, who is truth, fearless, timeless, and accessible through the Guru's grace.

verses.text: In the city of the body, there are many lamps. By the Guru's Light, the Divine Light is seen.
verses.explanation: This verse from Japji Sahib, the morning prayer of Guru Nanak, opens with Mool Mantra: 'Ik Onkar — There is One God.' It establishes the core Sikh belief in the oneness of the Creator, who is truth, fearless, timeless, and accessible through the Guru's grace.

chapters.title: Rahi Ras — The Evening Prayer (Guru Nanak)

verses.text: O Lord, we are Your sacrifice. We are Your servants. We worship You and meditate on Your name.
verses.explanation: This verse from the Rahi Ras (Evening Prayer) reminds the seeker to meditate on God's name at the close of day. It calls for gratitude and reflection on the divine presence that sustains all creation.

verses.text: The night is passing away and the morning dawn approaches. Let us sing the songs of the Lord's praises.
verses.explanation: This verse from the Rahi Ras (Evening Prayer) reminds the seeker to meditate on God's name at the close of day. It calls for gratitude and reflection on the divine presence that sustains all creation.

verses.text: The world is a dream. In a moment, it is gone. Only the Name of the Lord endures.
verses.explanation: This verse from the Rahi Ras (Evening Prayer) reminds the seeker to meditate on God's name at the close of day. It calls for gratitude and reflection on the divine presence that sustains all creation.

verses.text: The Lord's name is the cure for all sorrow. The Lord's name is the support of the soul.
verses.explanation: This verse from Sohila (the night prayer of Guru Arjan) is a bedtime hymn that prepares the soul for rest and ultimately for the final journey. It expresses trust in God's protection and mercy.

verses.text: Night and day, sing the Lord's praises. Through the Guru's Teachings, the Lord is found.
verses.explanation: This verse from Sohila (the night prayer of Guru Arjan) is a bedtime hymn that prepares the soul for rest and ultimately for the final journey. It expresses trust in God's protection and mercy.

verses.text: The Lord's light pervades all places. Through the Guru, the light is seen.
verses.explanation: This verse from Sohila (the night prayer of Guru Arjan) is a bedtime hymn that prepares the soul for rest and ultimately for the final journey. It expresses trust in God's protection and mercy.

chapters.title: Anand Sahib — The Song of Bliss (Guru Amar Das)

verses.text: By great good fortune, the Lord is met. By great good fortune, the Lord is found.
verses.explanation: This verse from Anand Sahib (Song of Bliss) by Guru Amar Das celebrates the spiritual ecstasy that comes from union with the Divine. True bliss is found in the company of the holy and in meditation on God's name.

verses.text: Bliss is in the Lord's name. Bliss is in the Lord's service.
verses.explanation: This verse from Anand Sahib (Song of Bliss) by Guru Amar Das celebrates the spiritual ecstasy that comes from union with the Divine. True bliss is found in the company of the holy and in meditation on God's name.

verses.text: Through the Guru, the Lord is known. Through the Guru, the Lord is found.
verses.explanation: This verse from Anand Sahib (Song of Bliss) by Guru Amar Das celebrates the spiritual ecstasy that comes from union with the Divine. True bliss is found in the company of the holy and in meditation on God's name.

chapters.title: Sukhmani Sahib — Psalm of Peace (Guru Arjan, Ashtpadi 1)

verses.text: Blessed, blessed is the Guru who reveals the Name of the Lord.
verses.explanation: This verse from Sukhmani Sahib (Psalm of Peace) by Guru Arjan teaches that peace comes from remembering God and living a life of virtue. The Lord dwells in those who are humble and free from ego.

verses.text: Those who are in love with the Lord, what can death do to them?
verses.explanation: This verse from Sukhmani Sahib (Psalm of Peace) by Guru Arjan teaches that peace comes from remembering God and living a life of virtue. The Lord dwells in those who are humble and free from ego.

verses.text: The Lord is the inner friend of all. He accompanies all through life and death.
verses.explanation: This verse from Sukhmani Sahib (Psalm of Peace) by Guru Arjan teaches that peace comes from remembering God and living a life of virtue. The Lord dwells in those who are humble and free from ego.

chapters.title: Sukhmani Sahib — Psalm of Peace (Guru Arjan, Ashtpadi 2)

verses.text: The body is the city, the senses are the gates, and the mind is the gatekeeper. Through the Guru's teachings, the True Lord comes to dwell within.
verses.explanation: This verse continues the Sukhmani Sahib's teachings on peace, emphasizing that inner stillness and contentment are found through the grace of the Guru and meditation on the divine Name.

verses.text: The mind is stained by worldly attachment. Through the Guru's teachings, it is cleansed.
verses.explanation: This verse continues the Sukhmani Sahib's teachings on peace, emphasizing that inner stillness and contentment are found through the grace of the Guru and meditation on the divine Name.

chapters.title: Wadhans Chhant — Hymns of the Lord (Guru Nanak)

verses.text: The Lord's name is the boat to cross the ocean of suffering. The Guru is the helmsman who steers the boat.
verses.explanation: This hymn in Wadhans Chhant by Guru Nanak expresses the soul's longing for the Divine Beloved. It teaches that without love and devotion, all worldly pleasures are empty.

verses.text: Those who meditate on the Lord's name are liberated. They swim across the ocean of pain.
verses.explanation: This hymn in Wadhans Chhant by Guru Nanak expresses the soul's longing for the Divine Beloved. It teaches that without love and devotion, all worldly pleasures are empty.

verses.text: Tell me, O Jogi, what is the highest religion? Tell me the essence of truth.
verses.explanation: This verse from Ramkali (Sidh Goshth) records Guru Nanak's discourse with the yogis. It affirms that true renunciation is inner detachment, not outward asceticism.

verses.text: The highest religion is to erase pride and conceit. The highest religion is to serve others.
verses.explanation: This verse from Ramkali (Sidh Goshth) records Guru Nanak's discourse with the yogis. It affirms that true renunciation is inner detachment, not outward asceticism.

verses.text: The soul-bride is colored with the Lord's love. She yearns to meet her Beloved.
verses.explanation: This verse in Basant rag by Guru Arjan celebrates the spring season as a metaphor for spiritual renewal. When the soul blooms with love for God, all seasons become joyful.

verses.text: Through the Guru's grace, the Beloved is found. The soul-bride is united with the Lord.
verses.explanation: This verse in Basant rag by Guru Arjan celebrates the spring season as a metaphor for spiritual renewal. When the soul blooms with love for God, all seasons become joyful.

verses.text: The world is transient. Why are you attached to it? The body will perish; only the Name endures.
verses.explanation: This hymn in Tilang rag by Guru Nanak speaks of the soul-bride's longing for the Divine Husband. It teaches that without the Guru's word, the soul wanders in separation and sorrow.

verses.text: Meditate on the Lord, O my mind. The Lord is the true support of all.
verses.explanation: This hymn in Tilang rag by Guru Nanak speaks of the soul-bride's longing for the Divine Husband. It teaches that without the Guru's word, the soul wanders in separation and sorrow.

chapters.title: Anand Sahib — The Song of Bliss (Guru Amar Das)

verses.text: I am Thine, O Lord, I am Thine; all mine are Thine, O Great King. My body, my breath, my wealth and my soul are Thine, O Lord; everything is Thine.
verses.explanation: This verse from Anand Sahib (Song of Bliss) by Guru Amar Das celebrates the spiritual ecstasy that comes from union with the Divine. True bliss is found in the company of the holy and in meditation on God's name.

verses.text: He is the treasure of virtue, the fountain of peace. He is the ocean of mercy, the form of bliss. He is the remover of pain, the bestower of peace.
verses.explanation: This verse from Anand Sahib (Song of Bliss) by Guru Amar Das celebrates the spiritual ecstasy that comes from union with the Divine. True bliss is found in the company of the holy and in meditation on God's name.

verses.text: The Guru has shown me the Lord's path, and wiped away my sorrows. Nanak, the servant, sings of the Lord's praises.
verses.explanation: This verse from Anand Sahib (Song of Bliss) by Guru Amar Das celebrates the spiritual ecstasy that comes from union with the Divine. True bliss is found in the company of the holy and in meditation on God's name.

chapters.title: Sukhmani Sahib — The Psalm of Peace (Guru Arjan)

verses.text: In the first quarter, meditate on the Lord, the Fearless One, who removes all fears. Remembering Him, your fears shall depart.
verses.explanation: This verse from Sukhmani Sahib (Psalm of Peace) by Guru Arjan teaches that the company of the saints is the highest blessing, purifying the mind and leading to liberation.

verses.text: O mind, why do you wander lost, without your Beloved? Apply yourself to the Guru's Word, and the Lord shall come to your home.
verses.explanation: This verse from Sukhmani Sahib (Psalm of Peace) by Guru Arjan teaches that the company of the saints is the highest blessing, purifying the mind and leading to liberation.

verses.text: He who has the Lord's blessing can never be harmed. His doubts are dispelled and his sorrows are removed.
verses.explanation: This verse from Sukhmani Sahib (Psalm of Peace) by Guru Arjan teaches that the company of the saints is the highest blessing, purifying the mind and leading to liberation.

verses.text: By singing the Lord's praises, one obtains supreme peace. The Guru's instruction is to meditate on the Lord's name.
verses.explanation: This verse from Sukhmani Sahib (Psalm of Peace) by Guru Arjan teaches that the company of the saints is the highest blessing, purifying the mind and leading to liberation.

chapters.title: Gauri — The Bride of Light (Guru Nanak)

verses.text: O Lord, through devotion I have found the jewel of Thy name. By Thy grace, I have obtained the treasure of the Guru's wisdom.
verses.explanation: This verse in Gauri rag by Guru Nanak uses the metaphor of the bride adorned for her husband to describe the soul preparing for union with the Divine. True beauty is spiritual virtue.

verses.text: The Lord's love is a deep ocean; how can I cross it? By the Guru's boat I cross the world ocean.
verses.explanation: This verse in Gauri rag by Guru Nanak uses the metaphor of the bride adorned for her husband to describe the soul preparing for union with the Divine. True beauty is spiritual virtue.

verses.text: There is but one God. True is His Name, creative His personality, and immortal His form.
verses.explanation: This hymn in Bilawal rag by Guru Nanak proclaims the equality of all humanity. The divine light shines equally in all beings; caste and creed are illusions.

verses.text: He is the same for all ages. He is beyond time. He is the Creator, without fear, without hate.
verses.explanation: This hymn in Bilawal rag by Guru Nanak proclaims the equality of all humanity. The divine light shines equally in all beings; caste and creed are illusions.

chapters.title: Maru — On the Nature of God (Guru Nanak)

verses.text: The Lord's name is the support of the earth. Without the Name, the world is wasted away.
verses.explanation: This verse in Maru rag by Guru Nanak reflects on the nature of God as infinite, unfathomable, and present everywhere. The Creator is in the creation and the creation is in the Creator.

verses.text: The Lord's ways are beyond human understanding. Through the Guru's grace, the mystery is revealed.
verses.explanation: This verse in Maru rag by Guru Nanak reflects on the nature of God as infinite, unfathomable, and present everywhere. The Creator is in the creation and the creation is in the Creator.

chapters.title: Gaund — The Power of God (Guru Nanak)

verses.text: The Lord's power is beyond measure. He sustains the universe and dwells within every heart.
verses.explanation: This hymn in Gaund rag by Guru Nanak celebrates the power and majesty of God. Nothing happens without His will; by surrendering to the divine will (hukam), one finds true peace.

verses.text: Without the Guru, no one can know the Lord. Through the Guru's Word, the path is illuminated.
verses.explanation: This hymn in Gaund rag by Guru Nanak celebrates the power and majesty of God. Nothing happens without His will; by surrendering to the divine will (hukam), one finds true peace.

verses.text: O mind, do not be proud of your wealth and youth. They shall pass away like the dew on the grass.
verses.explanation: This verse in Tukhari rag by Guru Nanak teaches the path of true renunciation. Leaving the world is not necessary; what matters is detachment from ego and attachment to God.

verses.text: Only the Name of the Lord is true; everything else is false. Meditate on the Lord, O my mind.
verses.explanation: This verse in Tukhari rag by Guru Nanak teaches the path of true renunciation. Leaving the world is not necessary; what matters is detachment from ego and attachment to God.
